postData.contentHtml:
For the first time in a decade, New Zealanders are more likely to view the United States as a threat than China, according to the latest ‘Perceptions of Asia and Asian Peoples (2026)’ survey by the Asia New Zealand Foundation. This striking reversal marks a significant moment in the nation’s foreign policy landscape, signaling a re-evaluation of its traditional alliances and a deepening engagement with its Asian neighbors.

The annual survey, which polled 2,300 New Zealanders aged 15 and over between January and February, found a considerable cooling of sentiment towards the United States. Friend perceptions of the US plummeted from 61 percent to 39 percent. Conversely, perceptions of China as a friend saw an increase, rising from 38 percent to 43 percent.

Experts suggest that recent global events and US actions may have influenced public opinion. David Capie, director of the Centre for Strategic Studies at Victoria University of Wellington, noted that the polling period coincided with controversial US incidents, potentially contributing to the decline in trust.

Beyond the US-China dynamic, the report reveals a broader trend: New Zealanders' trust in regional Asian powers, including Japan, India, Indonesia, China, and Australia, has steadily increased, while their trust in the United States continues its downward trajectory. Japan remains New Zealand's most trusted friend and defense partner in Asia, even surpassing the United Kingdom in trust for the first time. Singapore, the Philippines, and South Korea are also increasingly seen as close allies.

This shift isn’t merely political; it reflects a growing cultural and economic interconnectedness with Asia. An overwhelming 81 percent of New Zealanders believe developing ties with Asian countries is crucial for their nation’s future. Asia has become the most visited world region outside of Australia and the South Pacific, with travel to Asian destinations increasing by 14 percent, while trips to the US and Europe have declined. China, India, Indonesia, and Japan lead as top travel destinations.

Cultural engagement is also flourishing. New Zealanders are increasingly consuming Asian entertainment, with South Korean hits like the movie Parasite and the TV show Squid Game topping popularity charts. Cricket is the most followed sport from Asia, and a growing 60 percent of New Zealanders now report feeling connected to Asian cultures in their daily lives. This is further amplified by the increasing significance of Asian communities within New Zealand, particularly in metropolitan centers.

On the topic of immigration, over half of New Zealanders continue to view immigration from Asia positively, a figure that has risen by 12 percentage points over the last decade, despite a slight dip from last year. This positive sentiment underscores New Zealand’s evolving multicultural identity.

The findings of this comprehensive survey underscore a pivotal moment for New Zealand, as its people increasingly look towards Asia as a vital partner for its future, recalibrating traditional alliances and embracing a more diverse global outlook.
postData.title: A Seismic Shift: New Zealanders Now See US as Greater Threat Than China
postData.summary: A groundbreaking new poll reveals a dramatic reversal in New Zealand public opinion, with the United States now perceived as a greater threat than China. This unprecedented shift, detailed in the Asia New Zealand Foundation's annual survey, highlights declining trust in the US and a burgeoning connection with Asian nations across various spheres.

sources.title: New Zealand poll shows US seen as more of a threat than China
New Zealand sentiment towards Asia warms amid declining trust in US
